concept: Dog / The Companion at the Side
description: The dog: humanity's oldest companion animal, the one who chose us back. Coined by owner ruling — a language of community was incomplete without the species that invented interspecies community. The word carries both halves of the animal: 'lo-' reaching toward 'lothea' (love), and the falling 'a.u' of the howl, the wolf ancestry still audible at the end.
sound_symbolism: The first syllable leans toward 'lothea' (love); then 'ha' opens wide and 'u' falls away — a howl held in vowel hiatus. Stress lands on the open 'ha', the howl's peak. Say it slowly and let the last vowel drop: the ancestry is right there.
grammatical_notes: Noun: 'lohau nua mia thalo.' (The dog walks with me.) 'lohau lo lopia thema.' (The dog guards the children.) Vocatively, like a name: 'kona lohau.' (Here, dog.) Replaces the retired form hupo (2026-07), which named the pant but not the love.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: The dog is proof that trust can cross a species line and hold for ten thousand years — companionship as practiced, wordless nonviolence.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Herding, guarding, hauling, warming: the dog was the first coworker, paid in belonging — the oldest contract never written.
